evals.prompt: Implement the TaskLedger class in src/ledger/index.ts. It needs to hold tasks in-memory using a Map, and expose these methods: createTask(dto: CreateTaskDto): Task, claimTask(taskId: string, agentId: string): Result<Task>, completTask(taskId: string, output: TaskOutput): Result<Task>, and getPendingTasks(): Task[]. Status transitions should live in a separate src/ledger/transitions.ts as pure functions. Use the Zod schemas from src/types.ts for the Task type. This is for the AI agent workbench project.
evals.expected_output: A TaskLedger class in src/ledger/index.ts with correct TypeScript patterns: private Map, Result<T> return type for mutating ops, .js extensions on imports, import type for types, no enum usage, null-checked array access. A separate transitions.ts with pure transition functions.
expectations: TaskLedger is defined as a class with a private readonly Map<string, Task> field for storage
claimTask and completeTask return Result<Task>, not Task directly and not via throw
All import statements include .js file extensions (e.g. './types.js' not './types')
Type-only imports use 'import type' syntax (e.g. import type { Task })
No TypeScript enum keyword is used — uses z.enum() or 'as const' instead
No 'as TypeName' type assertions appear in the code
Status transition logic lives in a separate transitions.ts file as pure exported functions, not as TaskLedger methods
A Vitest test file exists that tests both the happy path and at least one error case for claimTask

evals.prompt: Add a `workbench status` CLI command to the workbench project. It should print all tasks in the ledger grouped by status (pending, in_progress, done, etc.), with each task showing its ID, title, and assigned persona. The command should support a --watch flag that refreshes every 2 seconds. Follow the project's Commander.js patterns with a factory function and separate view file.
evals.expected_output: A makeStatusCommand factory in src/commands/status.ts (thin handler), a renderStatusView function in src/views/status.view.ts (all console output here), and correct wiring. No business logic in the action handler. Proper .js imports, import type usage, no enums.
expectations: makeStatusCommand is exported as a factory function that accepts TaskLedger (or a service) as a parameter
The command's .action() handler contains no console.log or console.error calls — all output is delegated to a view
All console output lives in a separate view file (status.view.ts or similar)
The --watch flag is defined and triggers periodic refresh
The --json flag is defined for machine-readable output
All import statements include .js file extensions
TaskLedger is injected as a dependency parameter, not imported as a module-level singleton

evals.prompt: Implement the MCP tool handlers for claim_task and start_task in src/server/tools.ts. claim_task takes task_id and agent_id, transitions the task to claimed, and returns the updated task. start_task takes task_id and transitions claimed → in_progress. Both tools should validate their inputs with Zod schemas and handle errors gracefully so the calling agent can see and recover from them. The TaskLedger is injected.
evals.expected_output: registerTools function using server.registerTool() with Zod inputSchema, returning { isError: true } instead of throwing, delegating to ledger methods, no business logic in handlers. Correct .js imports, Result type handling.
expectations: Uses server.registerTool() from McpServer high-level API (not server.setRequestHandler())
Each registered tool passes a Zod schema as the inputSchema parameter
Error cases return { isError: true, content: [{ type: 'text', text: '...' }] } — no throw statements in tool handlers
At least claim_task and start_task are registered (and ideally submit_task, fail_task, get_task too)
Tool handlers delegate all state changes to the injected TaskLedger — no direct state mutation in handlers
All import statements include .js file extensions
No 'as TypeName' type assertions appear in the code
